proposition: The district court's fee award violates Fifth Circuit mandate requiring segregation of fees
cited_case: Vaughan v. Lewisville Indep. Sch. Dist., 62 F.4th 199, 207 (5th Cir. 2023)
retrieved_case: Vaughan v. Lewisville Indep Sch Dist
supporting_language: Vaughan requires on remand identifying fees 'incurred because' of sanctionable conduct, with findings that 'identify sanctionable conduct and distinguish it from the reasons for deciding the case on the merits' and 'link the sanctionable conduct to the size of the sanctions' (citing Procter & Gamble). But does not use 'segregation of fees' and context is narrow (deposition misconduct under 1927), not a broad mandate.

proposition: Sanctions order violates Rule 11(c)(2) and rests on post hoc justification barred by controlling precedent
cited_case: Cooter & Gell v. Hartmarx Corp., 496 U.S. 384 (1990)
supporting_language: Cooter & Gell addresses Rule 11 broadly but does not discuss Rule 11(c)(2) (created by 1993 amendments, 3 years after this 1990 decision) or 'post hoc justification.' Temporal-anchoring at *404 provides indirect support.

proposition: Sanctions order awards fees without causation segregation required by Fifth Circuit precedent
cited_case: Vaughan v. Lewisville Indep. Sch. Dist., 62 F.4th 199 (5th Cir. 2023)
retrieved_case: Vaughan v. Lewisville Indep Sch Dist
supporting_language: Vaughan requires 1927 sanctions tied to specific conduct: court 'may award only those fees and costs associated with the persistent prosecution of a meritless claim' and must identify fees 'incurred because' of misconduct. But context is deposition misconduct, not general 'causation segregation.' Proposition overstates.

proposition: Sanctions must not deter legitimate advocacy
cited_case: Coghlan v. Starkey, 852 F.2d 806, 809 (5th Cir. 1988)
supporting_language: Coghlan notes 'this court has no desire to deter any litigant from advancing any claim or defense arguably supported by existing law.' But context is Rule 38, and opinion's thrust is pro-sanctions.

proposition: Sanctions imposed sua sponte without notice or hearing implicate due process and are reviewed de novo
cited_case: Edwards v. Gen. Motors Corp., 153 F.3d 242, 246 (5th Cir. 1998)
supporting_language: Sanctions in Edwards were not sua sponte -- GM moved for fees and court held hearing. Court reviews 1927 sanctions for 'abuse of discretion,' not de novo. No discussion of de novo review for procedural deficiencies.

proposition: Morrison involved repeated filings after dispositive rulings and a clear record of delay (distinguishing from this case)
cited_case: Morrison v. Walker, 939 F.3d 633, 636-37 (5th Cir. 2019)
supporting_language: Opinion confirms repeated filings: 'he repeatedly made filings based only on the meritless falsified-perjury claim.' Brief uses Morrison as distinguishing case -- legitimate use.

proposition: Section 1927 does not reach the filing of a single non-frivolous pleading supported by evidence
cited_case: Peterson v. BMI Refractories, 124 F.3d 1386, 1396 (11th Cir. 1997)
supporting_language: Peterson reverses 1927 sanctions for lack of causal nexus. But does not hold 1927 'does not reach filing of single non-frivolous pleading.' Sanctioned conduct was pattern of communication failures.

proposition: Sanctions are an extraordinary remedy reserved for conduct that is both objectively unreasonable and undertaken in bad faith
cited_case: Morrison v. Walker, 939 F.3d 633, 637 (5th Cir. 2019)
supporting_language: Standard is 'reckless disregard of duty,' NOT 'bad faith.' Brief's 'bad faith' characterization overstates Morrison's holding -- reckless disregard is lower threshold.

proposition: Morrison involved repeated filings after dispositive rulings (distinguishing Bridgecrest's analogy)
cited_case: Morrison v. Walker, 939 F.3d 633, 637 (5th Cir. 2019)
supporting_language: Same factual support as Row 7. Opinion describes repeated filings after partial dismissal. Using Morrison as distinguishing case is accurate.

proposition: Section 1927 must not be used to punish counsel for pursuing claims that ultimately fail
cited_case: Morrison v. Walker, 939 F.3d 633, 637 (5th Cir. 2019)
supporting_language: Opinion does NOT state 1927 must not punish counsel for pursuing claims that ultimately fail. Opinion affirms sanctions against counsel precisely for pursuing a meritless claim.

proposition: Informal communications and draft motions do not satisfy Rule 11(c)(2)'s mandatory service requirement
cited_case: Deepwater Horizon v. Lake Eugenie Land & Dev., Inc., 857 F.3d 246, 250 (5th Cir. 2017)
retrieved_case: Freeh v. Lake Eugenie Land & Development, Inc.
supporting_language: This opinion (Freeh v. Lake Eugenie, 857 F.3d 246) is a Deepwater Horizon clawback fraud case. Contains NO discussion of Rule 11(c)(2), sanctions, or service requirements.

proposition: Courts must not act as advocates when imposing sanctions
cited_case: Deepwater Horizon v. Lake Eugenie Land & Dev., Inc., 857 F.3d 246, 250 (5th Cir. 2017)
retrieved_case: Freeh v. Lake Eugenie Land & Development, Inc.
supporting_language: Opinion (857 F.3d 246) does not address sanctions at all. Case concerns clawback of BP settlement funds.

proposition: The procedural safeguards required under section 1927 and the court's inherent powers were violated
cited_case: Edwards v. Gen. Motors Corp., 153 F.3d 242, 246 (5th Cir. 1998)
supporting_language: Court affirmed 1927 sanctions. Reversed only Rule 11 sanctions based on inapplicability to pre-removal state filings, not procedural deficiencies.

proposition: Government-issued fraud documentation triggers evidentiary presumption
cited_case: United States v. Miller, 953 F.3d 1095, 1101 (9th Cir. 2020)
supporting_language: United States v. Miller is a wire fraud/embezzlement case. No discussion of 'government-issued fraud documentation' or 'evidentiary presumption.' Word 'presumption' does not appear.

proposition: Frivolousness must be assessed at the time of filing not in hindsight
supporting_language: Fox does NOT address timing of frivolousness assessment. Opinion concerns fee allocation under 1988 but-for test. The brief's alleged quote 'The question is whether the case was frivolous when filed, not whether it later proved to be so' does NOT appear anywhere in the opinion.

proposition: Rule 11 sanctions are to be imposed only for arguments that are frivolous when made not for those that later prove unsuccessful
cited_case: Cooter & Gell v. Hartmarx Corp., 496 U.S. 384, 393 (1990)
supporting_language: Brief quotes at 393: 'Rule 11 sanctions are to be imposed only for arguments that are frivolous when made, not for those that later prove unsuccessful.' This exact quote does NOT appear. Page 393 discusses Rule 11 history.

proposition: Retroactive justification of sanctions is barred; contemporaneous scrutiny is required
supporting_language: Fox does NOT address retroactive justification or contemporaneous scrutiny. Opinion solely about allocating fees under 1988 when plaintiff asserts both frivolous and non-frivolous claims.

proposition: Retroactive justification of sanctions is barred; contemporaneous scrutiny is required
cited_case: Cooter & Gell v. Hartmarx Corp., 496 U.S. 384 (1990)
supporting_language: Does not use 'retroactive justification' or 'contemporaneous scrutiny.' Temporal-anchoring at *404 implies inquiry at filing time, but proposition is extrapolation, not direct holding.

proposition: Frivolousness must be judged at filing not reconstructed after the fact
supporting_language: Fox does NOT address timing of frivolousness. Court assumes claims already found frivolous and addresses only allocation. Proposition unsupported.

proposition: Frivolousness must be judged at filing not reconstructed after the fact
cited_case: Cooter & Gell v. Hartmarx Corp., 496 U.S. 384 (1990)
supporting_language: Supports temporal anchoring at *404 but language appears in context of appellate review standard, not standalone holding.

proposition: Sanctions imposed without formal notice briefing or evidentiary hearing constitute structural error
cited_case: Puckett v. United States, 556 U.S. 129, 135 (2009)
supporting_language: Puckett is about forfeited plea-agreement breach under Rule 52(b) plain-error review. Does not discuss sanctions at all.

proposition: Structural errors affect the framework within which the trial proceeds and are not subject to harmless error review
cited_case: Puckett v. United States, 556 U.S. 129, 135 (2009)
supporting_language: Phrase 'affect the framework within which the trial proceeds' does appear (quoting Fulminante). But at p.141 not p.135, and Court holds plea breach is NOT structural error -- brief inverts the holding.

proposition: Due process requires notice and an opportunity to be heard before sanctions are imposed
cited_case: Deepwater Horizon v. Lake Eugenie Land & Dev., Inc., 857 F.3d 246, 250 (5th Cir. 2017)
retrieved_case: Freeh v. Lake Eugenie Land & Development, Inc.
supporting_language: The quoted language 'Due process requires notice and an opportunity to be heard before sanctions are imposed' does NOT appear. Case is about Deepwater Horizon settlement clawback, not sanctions.

proposition: Sanctions imposed without formal notice briefing or evidentiary hearing are reviewed de novo because they implicate core constitutional safeguards
cited_case: Edwards v. Gen. Motors Corp., 153 F.3d 242, 246 (5th Cir. 1998)
supporting_language: Sanctions imposed after hearing on GM's motion. Court applied abuse of discretion: 'We review the ruling only for abuse of discretion.' No de novo review for procedural deficiencies discussed.

proposition: Courts must segregate fees incurred defending frivolous claims from those incurred defending non-frivolous ones
cited_case: Vaughan v. Lewisville Indep. Sch. Dist., 62 F.4th 199 (5th Cir. 2023)
retrieved_case: Vaughan v. Lewisville Indep Sch Dist
supporting_language: Opinion does NOT discuss segregating fees between frivolous and non-frivolous claims. Only one claim in Vaughan, found not frivolous. Remand instruction concerns fees from specific deposition misconduct, not segregation across claims of varying merit.

proposition: Fee recovery is limited to fees that would not have been incurred but for the frivolous claim
supporting_language: Core holding of Fox. At 836: 'Section 1988 permits the defendant to receive only the portion of his fees that he would not have paid but for the frivolous claim.'

proposition: Experian's silence distorted the compensable frame and undermined the adversarial process
supporting_language: Fox contains nothing about party silence, distortion of compensable frames, or adversarial process. Opinion is about fee allocation methodology.

proposition: Experian's silence distorted the compensable frame and undermined the adversarial process
cited_case: Vaughan v. Lewisville Indep. Sch. Dist., 62 F.4th 199, 207 (5th Cir. 2023)
retrieved_case: Vaughan v. Lewisville Indep Sch Dist
supporting_language: Opinion contains no discussion of 'silence distorting the compensable frame' or 'undermining the adversarial process.' Case is about VRA lawsuit frivolity and deposition misconduct. Nothing supports this proposition.

proposition: Defendants may receive only the portion of their fees that they would not have incurred but for the frivolous claim
supporting_language: Accurately states the holding. At 836: 'Section 1988 permits the defendant to receive only the portion of his fees that he would not have paid but for the frivolous claim.' Brief's quote is close paraphrase, substance accurate.

proposition: The district court must segregate fees incurred defending frivolous claims from those incurred defending non-frivolous ones
cited_case: Vaughan v. Lewisville Indep. Sch. Dist., 62 F.4th 199, 207 (5th Cir. 2023)
retrieved_case: Vaughan v. Lewisville Indep Sch Dist
supporting_language: Same as Row 26. Opinion does not hold courts 'must segregate fees incurred defending frivolous claims from those incurred defending non-frivolous ones.' Single non-frivolous claim; remand concerns deposition misconduct fees only.

proposition: The court awarded a flat percentage based on billing records violating the compensatory purpose of section 1927
cited_case: Vaughan v. Lewisville Indep. Sch. Dist., 62 F.4th 199, 207 (5th Cir. 2023)
retrieved_case: Vaughan v. Lewisville Indep Sch Dist
supporting_language: Opinion requires fees causally linked to sanctionable conduct: 'link the sanctionable conduct to the size of the sanctions.' Consistent with compensatory purpose, but does not discuss 'flat percentage based on billing records.'

proposition: Percentage-based fee allocations divorced from causation are rejected
cited_case: Hensley v. Eckerhart, 461 U.S. 424, 434-35 (1983)
supporting_language: At pp.434-435: 'work on an unsuccessful claim cannot be deemed to have been expended in pursuit of the ultimate result achieved.' Exact quote from opinion.

proposition: The fee award bundles unrelated litigation tasks and lacks line-item causation findings violating Fifth Circuit mandate
cited_case: Vaughan v. Lewisville Indep. Sch. Dist., 62 F.4th 199, 207 (5th Cir. 2023)
retrieved_case: Vaughan v. Lewisville Indep Sch Dist
supporting_language: Opinion requires findings that '(1) identify sanctionable conduct, (2) link the sanctionable conduct to the size of the sanctions, (3) differentiate between sanctions under different statutes' (citing Procter & Gamble). Supports causation findings, but never uses 'line-item causation findings.'

proposition: The fee award bundles unrelated litigation tasks and lacks line-item causation findings violating Supreme Court directive
supporting_language: Fox at 836 establishes but-for test requiring causal link. But does not use 'line-item causation findings' and Court says trial courts may 'use estimates in calculating and allocating an attorney's time' (at 838).

proposition: The court must determine which fees were incurred because of the frivolous claim
supporting_language: At 839: 'the trial court must determine whether the fees requested would not have accrued but for the frivolous claim.' Proposition accurately captures this.

proposition: The Fifth Circuit requires line-item segregation of fees caused by sanctionable conduct
cited_case: Vaughan v. Lewisville Indep. Sch. Dist., 62 F.4th 199, 207 (5th Cir. 2023)
retrieved_case: Vaughan v. Lewisville Indep Sch Dist
supporting_language: Opinion requires 1927 sanctions linked to specific conduct and identification of fees incurred 'because of' misconduct. Supports causation requirement, but never uses 'line-item segregation.'

proposition: Bulk allocation of 60% of total fees without isolating causation violates fee segregation requirements
cited_case: Vaughan v. Lewisville Indep. Sch. Dist., 62 F.4th 199, 207 (5th Cir. 2023)
retrieved_case: Vaughan v. Lewisville Indep Sch Dist
supporting_language: Opinion's requirement that sanctions be linked to specific conduct is inconsistent with bulk allocation. Underlying principle supported, but opinion does not address '60% bulk allocation.'

proposition: The absence of line-item segregation renders the award non-compensatory
cited_case: Vaughan v. Lewisville Indep. Sch. Dist., 62 F.4th 199, 207 (5th Cir. 2023)
retrieved_case: Vaughan v. Lewisville Indep Sch Dist
supporting_language: Opinion requires causal connection to sanctionable conduct. Consistent with compensatory nature, but does not use 'non-compensatory' or state absence of line-item segregation per se renders award invalid.

proposition: Sanctions must not be used as mere fee-shifting devices
cited_case: Cooter & Gell v. Hartmarx Corp., 496 U.S. 384, 397 (1990)
supporting_language: Opinion states Rule 11 is not fee-shifting (*409). But pinpoint at 397 is wrong -- page 397 discusses Rule 41(a)(1). Fee-shifting language at *409.

proposition: Sanctions under Rule 11 are collateral to the merits and may be imposed after dismissal
cited_case: Bryant v. Military Dep't of Miss., 597 F.3d 678, 682 (5th Cir. 2010)
retrieved_case: Bryant v. Military Department of Mississippi
supporting_language: Bryant addresses Rule 11/1927 on cross-appeal. But quoted proposition 'Sanctions under Rule 11 are collateral to the merits and may be imposed after dismissal' does not appear. Pinpoint at 682 refers to district court citation.

proposition: Fee awards must be causally tied to specific misconduct not merely proximity or shared docket
retrieved_case: Citizens Bank & Trust Co. v. Case (In re Case)
supporting_language: In re Case requires tying sanctions to specific conduct. But quoted language 'The court must carefully tie the award to the sanctionable conduct' does not appear verbatim.

proposition: An appeal is frivolous only if the result is obvious or the arguments of error are wholly without merit
cited_case: Coghlan v. Starkey, 852 F.2d 806, 811 (5th Cir. 1988)
supporting_language: At p.811: 'An appeal is frivolous if the result is obvious or the arguments of error are wholly without merit.' Core language directly from opinion. Pinpoint correct.

proposition: Section 1927 sanctions require evidence of bad faith improper motive or reckless disregard of duty
cited_case: Bryant v. Military Dep't of Miss., 597 F.3d 678, 694 (5th Cir. 2010)
retrieved_case: Bryant v. Military Department of Mississippi
supporting_language: At p.694: 1927 sanctions require 'evidence of bad faith, improper motive, or reckless disregard of duty owed to the court.' Pinpoint correct, proposition accurate.

proposition: Courts require a clear showing of bad faith for section 1927 sanctions not speculation or narrative
cited_case: Lewis v. Brown & Root, Inc., 711 F.2d 1287, 1292 (5th Cir. 1983)

supporting_language: Lewis at 1292 affirms 1927 award. But proposition about 'clear showing of bad faith' is not the standard -- case applies vexatious multiplication, not bad faith.

proposition: Section 1927 requires more than weak claims or procedural missteps; it demands a sustained course of vexatious litigation
cited_case: Matta v. May, 118 F.3d 410, 413 (5th Cir. 1997)
supporting_language: Matta reverses sanctions. Suit well-grounded in fact and law. Brief's characterization that Matta reversed where attorney filed one complaint without prolonging litigation is accurate.

proposition: Sanctions must not punish counsel for pursuing plausible claims
cited_case: Cooter & Gell v. Hartmarx Corp., 496 U.S. 384, 393 (1990)
supporting_language: Opinion notes Rule 11 'must be read in light of concerns that it will chill vigorous advocacy' (*393-394). Concept implicit but not stated as 'must not punish counsel for pursuing plausible claims.'

proposition: Sanctions order violates Rule 11(c)(2) and rests on post hoc justification barred by controlling precedent
supporting_language: Fox does not address Rule 11(c)(2) at all. Opinion concerns 1988 fee-shifting, not Rule 11 sanctions. Rule 11 never mentioned.

proposition: Sanctions order violates Rule 11(c)(2) and rests on post hoc justification barred by controlling precedent

proposition: Coghlan limits Rule 38 to objectively frivolous appeals

proposition: Sanctions must be grounded in objective evidence and articulated findings not frustration silence or narrative

proposition: Rule 11 discretion must be exercised within procedural bounds and anchored to articulated findings

proposition: The district court must carefully articulate the evidence and reasoning supporting its decision to impose sanctions

proposition: Discretion is not unbounded; it must be exercised within the framework of the applicable rules and precedents
